why: why did unhandled promise rejection happen
why use catch instead of the second argument to then
how: how to recover from a promise error

1_beginner_explanation.summary: In a Promise chain, if any step fails (either by calling `reject()` or throwing an error), the chain stops executing the remaining `.then()` blocks and 'falls down' to the nearest `.catch()` block. You only need one `.catch()` at the end of your chain to handle errors from any of the steps above it. This centralized error handling is a major advantage over callbacks, where you had to check for errors at every single step.
1_beginner_explanation.analogy: A Promise chain is like a staircase with a safety net at the bottom. If you trip on step 1, step 3, or step 5, you immediately fall straight down into the safety net (the `.catch()` block). You don't hit the intermediate steps on the way down.

2_internal_mechanism.execution_flow: When a Promise rejects, the engine looks at the next link in the chain. If it is a `.then(onFulfilled)`, the engine skips `onFulfilled`, creates a new rejected Promise, and passes the error to the next link. It repeats this skip-and-forward process until it finds a `.catch()` (or a `.then` with an `onRejected` second argument). The `.catch()` executes, and critically, it returns a new *resolved* Promise (unless it throws again), allowing the chain to continue.
2_internal_mechanism.memory: Unhandled rejections (a rejected Promise with no `.catch()`) trigger a global `unhandledrejection` event on the window/process. Modern Node.js environments will crash the application if this occurs.

3_real_world_example.use_case: Catching an error, logging it, and 'recovering' by returning a fallback value so the chain can continue successfully.

4_common_confusion.trap: Thinking a `try...catch` block around a Promise will catch its errors. It won't. `try...catch` only catches synchronous errors. The Promise executes asynchronously, long after the `try...catch` block has finished.
4_common_confusion.fix: For raw Promises, you must use `.catch()`. If you want to use `try...catch`, you must switch to `async/await` syntax.

6_edge_case.quirk: `.finally()` runs whether the Promise fulfilled OR rejected. It is perfect for cleanup logic (like hiding a loading spinner). Unlike `.then()`, whatever you return from `.finally()` is ignored — it simply passes the previous value or error further down the chain.
